questions.question: What does FHRP stand for?
questions.explanation: FHRP = First Hop Redundancy Protocol. It provides a redundant default gateway by having two or more routers share a virtual IP.

questions.question: What is the format of the HSRPv1 virtual MAC address?
questions.explanation: HSRPv1 VMAC is 0000.0C07.AC + group number (2 hex digits, supporting groups 0–255). HSRPv2 uses 0000.0C9F.F + 3 hex digits. VRRP uses 0000.5E00.01xx. GLBP uses 0007.B400.xxxx.

questions.question: What is the correct order of HSRP states?
questions.explanation: HSRP state progression is Init → Listen → Speak → Standby → Active. The active router answers for the virtual IP/MAC; standby is backup.

questions.question: What is the default HSRP priority, and what does preemption do?
options: 100; lets a higher-priority router take back active role after recovery
1; automatically starts HSRP on all interfaces
questions.explanation: Default HSRP priority is 100 (higher wins). Preemption allows a higher-priority router to take back the active role after recovery. HSRP is non-preemptive by default.

questions.question: Which FHRP is Cisco-proprietary and uses an active/standby model?
questions.explanation: HSRP is Cisco-proprietary with an active/standby model. VRRP is the open-standard equivalent (master/backup). GLBP is also Cisco-proprietary but load-balances within a subnet.

questions.question: What is the key load-balancing difference between HSRP/VRRP and GLBP?
options: HSRP/VRRP balance within a single subnet; GLBP balances across subnets only
HSRP/VRRP balance only across subnets (different active routers per group); GLBP balances within one subnet via multiple AVFs
questions.explanation: HSRP/VRRP achieve load-balancing only across subnets (different active router per group/VLAN). GLBP uniquely load-balances within a single subnet using up to 4 AVFs assigned by the AVG.

questions.question: What are AVG and AVF in GLBP?
options: Active Virtual Group, Active Virtual Forwarder
Active Virtual Gateway, Active Virtual Forwarder
Automatic Virtual Gateway, Automatic Virtual Forwarder
Active Voting Group, Active Voting Forwarder
questions.explanation: AVG (Active Virtual Gateway) is the single elected router per subnet that assigns VMAC addresses. AVF (Active Virtual Forwarder) is one of up to four forwarders that actually forward traffic.

questions.question: When the active router fails and the standby takes over, what Layer 2 mechanism updates switch MAC tables?
options: Gratuitous ARP sent by the new active router
questions.explanation: The new active router sends a gratuitous ARP (unrequested ARP broadcast) so switches relearn the virtual MAC's new location — ensuring fast failover.

questions.question: Which command configures a virtual IP 172.16.0.254 in HSRP group 1?
questions.explanation: `standby 1 ip 172.16.0.254` configures the virtual IP for HSRP group 1 on the interface. HSRP commands use the `standby` keyword on Cisco IOS.

questions.question: What is required to run HSRPv2 instead of HSRPv1 on an interface?
questions.explanation: `standby version 2` enables HSRPv2, which is required for IPv6 support and expands group range from 0–255 (v1) to 0–4095 (v2).

questions.question: Which multicast address does HSRPv2 use?
questions.explanation: HSRPv2 uses 224.0.0.102 (same address GLBP uses). HSRPv1 uses 224.0.0.2. VRRP uses 224.0.0.18.

questions.question: If two HSRP routers have equal priority, what breaks the tie to elect the active router?
questions.explanation: With equal HSRP priority, the highest interface IP address wins and becomes active. Priority (default 100, higher wins) is checked first; IP is the tiebreaker.
